questions.question: Explain the difference between static and dynamic memory allocation. How does this relate to arrays and linked lists?
keyPoints: Static allocation happens at compile time, dynamic at runtime
Arrays use static/fixed contiguous memory
Linked lists use dynamic allocation (malloc/free)
Dynamic allows growth/shrink during execution

questions.question: Describe the structure of a node in a singly linked list. Write its declaration in C.
keyPoints: Node has two parts: data and next pointer
Data stores the actual element
Next pointer stores address of next node
Self-referential structure definition

questions.question: What are the advantages and disadvantages of linked lists over arrays?
keyPoints: Advantages: dynamic size, efficient insertion/deletion at beginning, no memory wastage
Disadvantages: no random access, extra memory for pointers, not cache-friendly
Trade-off between flexibility and direct access

questions.question: Write an algorithm to insert a new node at the beginning of a singly linked list.
keyPoints: Create new node using malloc
Set new node's data
Set new node's next to current head
Update head to point to new node
Time complexity O(1)

questions.question: Explain the concept of a circular linked list. What are its applications?
keyPoints: Last node points back to first node
No NULL termination
Types: singly circular and doubly circular
Applications: round-robin scheduling, circular buffers, repeated operations

questions.question: How is a polynomial represented using linked lists? Explain with an example.
keyPoints: Each term stored as node with coefficient and exponent
Nodes arranged in descending order of exponents
Only non-zero terms stored (sparse polynomial)
Efficient for polynomial addition/multiplication

questions.question: What is a doubly linked list? Explain its advantages over singly linked list.
keyPoints: Each node has prev and next pointers
Allows bidirectional traversal
Can traverse backwards easily
Easier deletion with access to previous node
Uses more memory (extra pointer per node)

questions.question: Write a C program to implement a singly linked list with functions for: (i) Creation (ii) Insertion at beginning (iii) Insertion at end (iv) Deletion from beginning (v) Display/Traversal
keyPoints: Define node structure with data and next pointer
Create function allocates memory and initializes
Insert beginning: update head pointer
Insert end: traverse to last node, then add
Delete beginning: move head to next, free old head
Display: traverse from head, print data until NULL
Time complexities mentioned
Proper memory management with free()

questions.question: Explain the implementation of a stack using linked list. Write functions for push and pop operations. Compare it with array-based stack.
keyPoints: Linked stack uses head as top of stack
Push: insert at beginning (O(1))
Pop: delete from beginning (O(1))
No overflow (unless heap exhausted) vs fixed size array stack
Dynamic size vs fixed size
Extra memory for pointers
Both O(1) for push/pop

questions.question: Explain the implementation of a queue using linked list. Write functions for enqueue and dequeue operations.
keyPoints: Maintain two pointers: front and rear
Enqueue: insert at rear (O(1) with rear pointer)
Dequeue: delete from front (O(1) with front pointer)
Handle empty queue case
Update both pointers appropriately
Comparison with array-based circular queue

questions.question: Write an algorithm to reverse a singly linked list. Explain the approach and its time/space complexity.
keyPoints: Three pointers approach: prev, current, next
Initialize prev = NULL, current = head
While current is not NULL: store next, reverse pointer, move prev and current
At end, prev becomes new head
Time complexity: O(n)
Space complexity: O(1) - only pointers used
